title: Stylidium cilium
taxonomy: Stylidium cilium is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Stylidiaceae, commonly known as trigger plants or triggerworts. This family is an endemic group of plants found primarily in Australia, with some species also found in New Zealand and South America. The Stylidiaceae family is a part of the order Asterales, which is a large group of flowering plants that also includes daisies, sunflowers, and asters. Stylidium cilium is further classified as a member of the genus Stylidium, which contains over 300 species of trigger plants. The specific classification of Stylidium cilium is Stylidium cilium (S.W.Tulloch & B.J.Keely), which is a combination of its genus name, species name, and the names of the botanists who first described it.

intro: Stylidium cilium, commonly known as the 'hairy triggerplant', is a unique and fascinating plant species that belongs to the Stylidiaceae family, characterized by its distinctive trigger-like mechanism for pollination. Stylidium cilium has been traditionally used in Australian Indigenous medicine to treat various health issues, including inflammation, wounds, and skin conditions, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of Stylidium cilium include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made from Stylidium cilium include teas, infusions, and topical creams, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including eczema, acne, and minor wounds.
